properties.name: Thingwall (Wirral)
properties.etymology: Old Norse þing-vǫllr, 'assembly field'. The meeting place of the Norse community of Wirral — a direct parallel to Þingvellir in Iceland and Tynwald on the Isle of Man.
properties.notes: Strongest single piece of evidence for an autonomous Norse enclave on Wirral after Ingimund's settlement c.902.

properties.name: Thingwall (Liverpool)
properties.etymology: Old Norse þing-vǫllr, 'assembly field'. A second thing-site east of Liverpool, likely serving the Norse settlers between Mersey and Ribble.
properties.notes: Survives in Thingwall Road / Thingwall Hall, Knotty Ash. Two assembly sites either side of the Mersey imply substantial organised settlement.

properties.name: West Kirby
properties.etymology: Old Norse kirkju-býr, 'village with a church'. 'West' distinguishes it from the lost Kirkby in Wallasey.
properties.notes: St Bridget's church dedication suggests Norse-Irish settlers from Dublin.

properties.name: Kirkby
properties.etymology: Old Norse kirkju-býr, 'village with a church'.

properties.name: Kirkdale
properties.etymology: Old Norse kirkju-dalr, 'church valley'.

properties.name: Ormskirk
properties.etymology: Old Norse Orms-kirkja, 'Ormr's church' — from the Norse personal name Ormr ('serpent').

properties.name: Irby
properties.etymology: Old Norse Íra-býr, 'settlement of the Irish' — probably Norse settlers arriving from Ireland after the 902 expulsion from Dublin.
properties.notes: Direct toponymic evidence of the Hiberno-Norse character of the Wirral colony.

properties.name: Frankby
properties.etymology: Old Norse Franka-býr, 'Franki's farmstead' (or 'farm of the Frank/Frenchman').

properties.name: Greasby
properties.etymology: Originally Old English græfe-burh ('stronghold by a grove'); the ending was remodelled to Norse -býr by Scandinavian speakers.
properties.notes: Example of Norse speech influencing an existing English name — evidence of language contact, not new settlement.

properties.name: Pensby
properties.etymology: Brittonic penn ('hill') + Old Norse býr — a Celtic hill-name adopted by Norse settlers.
properties.notes: Three languages in one name: Welsh hill, Norse farm.

properties.name: Raby
properties.etymology: Old Norse rá-býr, 'boundary settlement' — likely marking the edge of the Norse enclave on Wirral.
properties.notes: Its position supports the theory of a defined Norse territory with a negotiated border.

properties.name: Whitby
properties.etymology: Old Norse hvíta-býr, 'white farmstead'.

properties.name: Helsby
properties.etymology: Old Norse hjalli-býr, 'farmstead at the ledge' — referring to Helsby Hill.
properties.notes: Southern-most of the Wirral-area -bý names, on the Mersey's south bank.

properties.name: Crosby (Great Crosby)
properties.etymology: Old Norse krossa-býr, 'settlement with crosses' — implying a Christian (likely Hiberno-Norse) population.

properties.name: Little Crosby
properties.etymology: Old Norse krossa-býr, 'settlement with crosses'; the smaller twin of Great Crosby.

properties.name: Formby
properties.etymology: Old Norse Forna-býr, 'Forni's farmstead', or fornr býr, 'old farmstead'.

properties.name: Roby
properties.etymology: Old Norse rá-býr, 'boundary settlement' — same formation as Raby on Wirral.

properties.name: West Derby
properties.etymology: Old Norse djúra-býr, 'deer settlement/park'. 'West' distinguishes it from Derby in the East Midlands.
properties.notes: Royal manor and head of the West Derby hundred — the administrative heart of south-west Lancashire long before Liverpool mattered.

properties.name: Ribby
properties.etymology: Old Norse hryggr-býr, 'farmstead on a ridge'.
properties.notes: Fylde outlier — shows the -bý zone continuing north of the Ribble.

properties.name: Westby
properties.etymology: Old Norse vestr-býr, 'western farmstead'.

properties.name: Caldy
properties.etymology: Domesday 'Calders' — possibly Old English calu-dūn ('bald hill'); Norse influence on the modern form is debated.
properties.notes: Included for completeness; weakest of the Wirral set.

properties.name: Toxteth
properties.etymology: Old Norse Tóka-stǫð, 'Tóki's landing-place' — a beaching point on the Mersey shore.

properties.name: Croxteth
properties.etymology: Old Norse Króka-stǫð, 'Krókr's landing-place' — on the River Alt.
properties.notes: Paired with Toxteth: two named Norse landing-places on Liverpool's rivers.

properties.name: Meols
properties.etymology: Old Norse melr, 'sandbank / sandhills'.
properties.notes: One of Britain's most important Viking-age beach-market sites: thousands of finds including Hiberno-Norse metalwork. Probably the port of entry for the 902 settlers.

properties.name: Tranmere
properties.etymology: Old Norse trani-melr, 'crane-bird sandbank'.

properties.name: Ravenmeols
properties.etymology: Old Norse Hrafns-melr, 'Hrafn's sandhills'.
properties.notes: Medieval village largely lost beneath the Formby dunes.

properties.name: Argarmeols (lost)
properties.etymology: Old Norse, probably Ergs-melr or a personal name + melr, 'sandhills'. A Domesday vill lost to the sea by the 14th century.
properties.notes: Lost settlement — location approximate, somewhere under or off the Birkdale shore.

properties.name: Ainsdale
properties.etymology: Old Norse Einulfs-dalr, 'Einulfr's valley' (or dale-share).

properties.name: Birkdale
properties.etymology: Old Norse birki-dalr, 'birch valley'.

properties.name: Skelmersdale
properties.etymology: Old Norse Skjaldmarrs-dalr, 'Skjaldmarr's valley'.

properties.name: Aigburth
properties.etymology: Old Norse eiki-berg, 'oak-tree hill'.

properties.name: Litherland
properties.etymology: Old Norse hlíðar-land, 'land on a slope'.

properties.name: Aintree
properties.etymology: Old Norse ein-tré, 'lone tree'.

properties.name: Lunt
properties.etymology: Old Norse lundr, 'grove' — a word with pagan sacred-grove associations elsewhere in the Norse world.

properties.name: Burscough
properties.etymology: Old Norse buri-skógr, 'wood by the fort' (OE burh + ON skógr).

properties.name: Scarisbrick
properties.etymology: Old Norse Skars-brekka, 'Skár's slope'.

properties.name: Great Altcar
properties.etymology: River-name Alt (Brittonic) + Old Norse kjarr, 'brushwood marsh'.

properties.name: Hesketh Bank
properties.etymology: Old Norse hesta-skeið, 'horse race-track' — one of several such names in the Norse west.
properties.notes: Horse-racing was a documented Norse pastime; the name implies a gathering place.

properties.name: Crossens
properties.etymology: Old Norse kross-nes, 'headland with a cross'.

properties.name: Ness
properties.etymology: Old Norse nes or Old English næss, 'headland' — the two are indistinguishable here.

properties.name: Arrowe
properties.etymology: Old Norse ærgi, 'shieling / summer pasture' — itself borrowed from Old Irish áirge. A hallmark of Norse settlers who had lived in the Gaelic world.

properties.name: Anglezarke
properties.etymology: Old Norse Anlafs-erg, 'Anlaf's (Óláfr's) shieling' — Norse personal name + Gaelic-derived pasture word.
properties.notes: Eastern edge of the corridor — upland grazing named by Hiberno-Norse farmers.

properties.name: Grimsargh
properties.etymology: Old Norse Gríms-erg, 'Grímr's shieling'.

properties.name: Goosnargh
properties.etymology: Old Irish personal name Gosan + Old Norse erg, 'Gosan's shieling' — a Gaelic name in a Norse formation.

properties.name: Noctorum
properties.etymology: Old Irish cnocc-tírim, 'dry hill' — a purely Gaelic name, presumably given by Irish speakers among the Norse settlers.
properties.notes: With Irby and Liscard, evidence that the 902 settlers were a mixed Norse-Irish community.

properties.name: Liscard
properties.etymology: Old Irish lios na carraige, 'court/enclosure at the rock'.

properties.name: Becconsall
properties.etymology: Old Irish personal name Bekan + Old Norse haugr, 'Bekan's mound' — possibly a burial mound.

properties.name: Thurstaston
properties.etymology: Norse personal name Þorsteinn + Old English tūn: 'Thorstein's farmstead'. A Norse landowner in an English naming pattern.

properties.name: Storeton
properties.etymology: Old Norse stórr ('great') + Old English tūn: 'great farmstead'.

properties.name: Larton
properties.etymology: Old Norse leirr ('clay') + Old English tūn: 'clay farmstead'.

properties.name: Gayton
properties.etymology: Old Norse geit ('goat') + Old English tūn: 'goat farm'.

properties.name: Claughton
properties.etymology: Old Norse klakkr ('hillock') + Old English tūn.

properties.name: Tarleton
properties.etymology: Norse personal name Þaraldr + Old English tūn: 'Tharald's farmstead'.
